coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon quantum dots as an optical sensor for selective fluorescent determination of promethazine hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Ensafi, Ali A.
Nasr-Esfahani, Parisa
Rezaei, B.
coredata.dc:description: An optical sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) was fabricated for selective and sensitive determination of promethazine hydrochloride (PrHy). Water-soluble and fluorescent CQDs were synthesized by a simple, low cost and green approach using orange juice as a carbon source. The special advantage of the synthesized CQDs with this method is low toxicity. The surface of the CQDs was covered with MIPs matrix (CQDs-MIPs) via sol-gel polymerization. For characterization of the CQDs and CQDs-MIPs, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering technique, X-ray diffraction technique, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy were used. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs exhibited linear response with the concentration of PrHy in the range from 2.0 to 250μmolL−1 with a detection limit of 0.5μmolL−1. The reproducibility of the method was checked for 20μmolL−1 of PrHy, and the results showed RSD% as 5.1. This sensor was used for determination of PrHy in real samples and showed satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Sol-gel polymerization
Promethazine hydrochloride

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs from orange juice
Characterization of CQDs, CQDs-MIPs, and CQDs-NIPs
Effect of the amount of CQDs-MIPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A): UV–vis spectra of CQDs. (B): Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. and (C): Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs and CQDs-MIPs at 330nm as excitation wavelength.
(A): DLS analysis of the CQDs. (B): XRD pattern of the CQDs.
(A): TEM image of (I): CQDs, (II): CQDs-MIPs, and (III): CQDs-NIPs. DLS analysis of (B): CQDs-MIPs, and (C): CQDs-NIPs. (D): FT-IR spectra of CQDs, CQDs-MIPs, and CQDs-NIPs.
(A): UV–vis spectra of extraction mixtures after each wash and (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-MIPs after each wash.
(A): UV–vis spectra of PrHy and CQDs-MIPs, (B) UV–vis spectra of PrHy and fluorescence spectrum of CQDs-MIPs.

(A): Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs, Conditions: 400mgL−1 of CQDs-MIPs, 60μmolL−1 of PrHy at different pH. (B): Effect of the amount of CQDs-MIPs on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs. Conditions: 60μmolL−1 of PrHy, at pH 7.0 with different amount of CQDs-MIPs.

(A) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs. Conditions: 600mgL−1 of CQDs-MIPs, 60μmolL−1 of PrHy at pH 7.0. (B) Effect of time on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs; Conditions: 600mgL−1 of CQDs-MIPs, 60μmolL−1 of PrHy at pH 7.0.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) CQDs, (B) CQDs-NIPs and (C) CQDs-MIPs, at the optimum conditions in the presence of different concentrations of PrHy as 0.0, 2.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100.0, 120.0, 140.0, 160.0, 180.0, 200.0, 220.0 and 250.0μmolL−1.

(A) The Stern–Volmer curve, and (B) calibration curve for PrHy at the optimum conditions with different concentrations of PrHy as 0.0, 2.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100.0, 120.0, 140.0, 160.0, 180.0, 200.0, 220.0 and 250.0μmolL−1.
The responses of CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs to 0.6mmolL−1 different species in the presence of 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy at the optimum conditions.
Results of recovery test in human blood plasma samples (n=3).
Comparison of different methods for the quantification of PrHy.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon quantum dots as an optical sensor for selective fluorescent determination of promethazine hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optical sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) was fabricated for selective and sensitive determination of promethazine hydrochloride (PrHy). Water-soluble and fluorescent CQDs were synthesized by a simple, low cost and green approach using orange juice as a carbon source. The special advantage of the synthesized CQDs with this method is low toxicity. The surface of the CQDs was covered with MIPs matrix (CQDs-MIPs) via sol-gel polymerization. For characterization of the CQDs and CQDs-MIPs, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering technique, X-ray diffraction technique, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy were used. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs exhibited linear response with the concentration of PrHy in the range from 2.0 to 250μmolL−1 with a detection limit of 0.5μmolL−1. The reproducibility of the method was checked for 20μmolL−1 of PrHy, and the results showed RSD% as 5.1. This sensor was used for determination of PrHy in real samples and showed satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanoparticles with diameters between 2 and 10nm [1]. The discovery of CQDs was down during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through permeative electrophoresis by Xu et al. [2]. CQDs have emerged as potential rivals of semiconductor quantum dots. Semiconductor quantum dots have high toxicity due to the use of heavy metals in their production. CQDs have attracted much attention because of their benefits compared with semiconductor quantum dots. The unique properties of CQDs include high stability, low toxicity, simple synthetic routes, environmental friendliness and good biocompatibility [2]. CQDs, especially due to their tunable and strong fluorescence emission properties, are widely used in biomedicine [3], catalysis [4] and sensing [5]. So far, several methods for the synthesis of CQDs has been developed such as microwave [6], electrochemical [7], chemical oxidation [8], hydrothermal treatment [9] and ultrasonic methods [10]. To control the physicochemical properties of CQDs, surface passivation and functionalization are performed. For self-passivating, CQDs are synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization with high temperature or microwave assisted with different carbon precursors. Recently synthesis of CQDs with a hydrothermal method known as a facile, efficient and green route. In the green synthesis of CQDs, natural biomass precursors are used as raw materials. The advantage of this approach is that avoid the using a large amount of toxic and expensive chemicals as well as intricate processes after synthesis is not required [11].

Molecularly imprinting is used as a technique for the preparation of polymers with recognition sites of molecules. So far, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have prepared on the surface of QDs such as CdTe QDs [12], CdSe QDs [13], CQDs [14] and graphene QDs [15]. Because CQDs are poor in terms of selectivity, they should be modified with suitable methods. Molecular imprinting is a simple and low-cost method for modification of CQDs [16]. In this method, the surface of CQDs coated with MIPs. MIPs have recognition sites that regarding size, shape and functional group are similar to the template molecules. Recognition cavities are produced within the polymer structure by the copolymerization of functional monomers and cross linkers in the presence of template molecules [17]. In order to synthesis of MIPs, different ways such as precipitation polymerization, bulk polymerization, suspension polymerization, multi-step swelling polymerization, and sol-gel polymerization are provided [14]. Among these ways, sol-gel is the most interesting method because of having advantages such as easier to extract template molecules, smaller particle size, larger surface area, higher binding capacity and faster mass transfer [18].

Promethazine hydrochloride (PrHy, N,N-dimethyl-1-phenothiazine-10-yl propan-2-amine hydrochloride) is an antihistamine that used to treat allergy symptoms such as rash, itching, and runny nose. It is also used to prevent and treat nausea and vomiting related to certain conditions (such as before/after surgery and motion sickness). Some of the PrHy side effects are endocrinal, cardiac and reproductive alterations [19]. So far, various methods have been reported for the determination of PrHy such as electrochemical methods [20], liquid chromatography [21], electrophoresis [22], and spectrofluorimetry [23]. Among these various methods, fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted a lot of attention due to low cost, simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity.

In the current work, an optical sensor based on MIPs on the surface of CQDs (CQDs-MIPs) was fabricated for determination of PrHy. CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using orange juice as a carbon source. In the following, MIPs were deposited on the surface of CQDs via sol-gel method. In the synthesis of MIPs, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) as a functional monomer, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as a cross-linker and PrHy as the template molecule were used. In this method, high selectivity of MIPs and high sensitivity of fluorescence CQDs were combined, therefore a simple, rapid, selective and sensitive method for determination of PrHy was created.

Oranges were bought from the local market and before use processed into juice using an electrical fruit juicer. Promethazine hydrochloride was purchased from Amin Pharmaceutical Company (Isfahan, Iran). Triton X-100, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), ammonium hydroxide solution (25–28%), cyclohexane, acetone, ethanol, acetonitrile and trichloroacetic acid were purchazed from Aldrich (London, UK). 0.10molL−1 H3PO4 and NaOH solution were used for preparing phosphate buffer solutions. Doubly distilled water was used for the preparation of all solutions.

All fluorescence spectra were taken by a Jasco FP–750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). A Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used for recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra on KBr plate (at 4000–400cm−1). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a double beam Jasco V–570 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was done on a Philips CM30 300kV TEM operating at 300kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on a Bruker D8/Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern ZEN 3600, UK) was used for obtaining particle size distribution of the CQDs. A pH-meter (Corning, Model 140) with a double junction glass electrode was used to measure the acidity of the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from orange juice
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized from orange juice as a source of carbon using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 40mL of orange juice without pulp was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 240min. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The resulting dark brown suspension was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min to remove large particle. The solution was stored in a suitable container and kept at 4°C. In fact, CQDs is formed due to carbonization of the major constituents orange juice such as sucrose, glucose, fructose, citric acid and ascorbic acid.

For the synthesis of CQDs-MIPs, the reverse microemulsion method was used [12]. In the preparation of CQDs-MIPs, 1.8mL of Triton X-100, 5.0mg of PrHy, 50μL of TEOS and 20μL of APTES were added to 7.5mL of cyclohexane under magnetic stirring. The mixture was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30min. Then, 1.0mL CQDs and 100μL of ammonium hydroxide (25–28% v/v) was added to the above mixture. Afterward, the mixture was stirred for 12h at room temperature. Finally, 20mL of acetone was used for breaking the microemulsion. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 5min. The product was washed with a mixture of ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2v/v) to remove the template molecule. At the end, CQDs-MIPs was dried in the oven at 80°C for 60min.
The non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) on the surface of CQDs (CQDs-NIPs) were synthesized under the above conditions without addition of the PrHy.

3.0mg of the CQDs-MIPs was dispersed into 5.0mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) by ultrasonic vibration. Then, a certain volume of PrHy standard solution was added to the above mixture. For recording the fluorescence spectra, the excitation wavelength was set at 330nm, and the spectral range was selected between 350 and 600nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded in the absence and presence of PrHy and assigned as F0 and F, respectively. The values of F0–F were obtained in different concentrations of PrHy.

Blood plasma samples of human were received from the Isfahan University of Technology Health Center. 2.0mL of plasma sample, 2.0mL of 10% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid and various amounts of standard PrHy solutions were mixed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged for 20min at 5000rpm, to precipitate any proteins. The top phase was filtered and ten-fold diluted with the phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). Then, for PrHy detection, 300μL of the real sample was added to 5mL of the suspension containing 3.0mgL−1 of the CQDs-MIPs at pH 7.0, using the recommended procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, CQDs-MIPs, and CQDs-NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. It can be seen that the spectrum has a sharp peak at 306nm, which is probably related to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond [24].

The fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400nm, is shown in Fig. 1B. The maximum of the fluorescence emission peak showed intensity reduction with the red shift from 404 to 478nm. This observation proves that the fluorescence emission of the CQDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength. Because the maximum of fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs was observed at 330nm excitation wavelength so, this wavelength was chosen as an excitation wavelength for all experiments. Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs and CQDs-MIPs at 330nm (as an excitation wavelength) are shown in Fig. 1C. The fluorescence emission peaks of CQDs and CQDs-MIPs are located at 409nm and 431nm, respectively. This red-shift is happened due to the increasing the size of CQDs during the polymerization of TEOS and APTES on the surface of the CQDs.

To obtain the average diameter and particle size distribution of the CQDs, DLS technique was used. DLS diagram of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 A. As can be seen, the size distribution of CQDs is between 3.2–4.9nm. Also, low polydispersity index (PDI) value (0.189) confirms a narrow size distribution of the CQDs.
The XRD pattern of CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2B. A broad peak at around 2Ɵ=20.4 that corresponded to the (002) diffraction planes of carbon materials reveals an amorphous structure in the CQDs.

The morphologies of CQDs, CQDs-MIPs, and CQDs-NIPs were studied by TEM and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. As can be seen, CQDs have a spherical shape with an average diameter about 5nm. Also, the images of CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs showed spherical structures with approximately 60nm diameter. This increase in size is due to the aggregation of the particles and formation of silica coating on the surface of the CQDs during the preparation of CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs. Also DLS diagram of the CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs are shown in Fig. 3B and C, respectively. As can be seen, the average sizes of CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs are 62.1 and 64.5nm, respectively. The higher PDI values of CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs (0.391 and 0.362, respectively) than CQDs (0.189) shows that CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs have broader size distributions than CQDs.

FT–IR spectra of CQDs, CQDs-MIPs, and CQDs-NIPs are shown in Fig. 3D. For CQDs, a broad absorption band at 3389cm−1 is related to the OH stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 2925, 1734, 1611, 1522 and 1197cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CH, CO, CC, CN and CO groups. The peaks at 1026cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of COC group. For CQDs-MIPs and CQDs-NIPs the FT–IR peaks around 1052 and 783cm−1 are oriented from asymmetric stretching of SiOSi and bending vibration of the SiO groups, respectively. The bands around 3271 and 2930cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations CH and NH, respectively. These observations suggested that the MIPs was successfully formed on the surface of the CQDs.

During the synthesis of CQDs-MIPs, Triton X-100, cyclohexane and ammonium hydroxide were used as a surfactant, a continuous phase, and a catalyst, respectively. Also, APTES, TEOS, and PrHy were utilized as a functional monomer, a cross-linker and a template molecule, respectively. In this method, Triton X-100 diffused and stabilize water droplets containing CQDs in cyclohexane. Because of silica is a suitable coating for protecting the CQDs and enhancing the reaction between CQDs and imprinted polymers, silica layer should be coated on the CQDs before the formation of MIPs [14]. Silica layer on the surface of CQDs was formed by hydrolyzing TEOS that catalyzed with ammonium hydroxide. The MIPs were created via copolymerization of the APTES and TEOS in the presence of PrHy. During the polymerization reaction, hydrogen bonds between APTES and PrHy were formed. Consequently, PrHy was successfully embedded on the silica-coated matrix at the surface of CQDs. During the washing, the template molecules were removed and the CQDs-MIPs with recognition sites of PrHy were obtained.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the extraction mixtures, after each washing, are shown in Fig. 4 A. As can be seen, after 6 times washing, PrHy is not detectable by the UV–vis spectrophotometer. Also, Fig. 4B confirms that before removal of PrHy, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs was only about 5% that of the CQDs-NIPs. On the other hand, after 6 times washing with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2v/v), the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-MIPs was recovered to about 95% that of the CQDs-NIPs. These results suggested the facile and effective removal of PrHy from CQDs-MIPs.

The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs was quenched by rebinding PrHy. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching may involve charge transfer or energy transfer from CQDs-MIPs to PrHy [25,26]. As revealed by the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5 A), the UV absorption spectrum of PrHy was close to the band gap of the CQDs-MIPs. On the other hand, as can be seen in Fig. 5B the UV absorption spectrum of PrHy was away from the emission spectrum of the CQDs-MIPs. The charges in the conduction bands of the CQDs-MIPs could transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the PrHy. We excluded energy transfer as a possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching because there was no spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of the PrHy and the emission spectra of the CQDs-MIPs. This results indicated that the charge transfer from the CQDs-MIPs to PrHy was responsible for the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

For investigation the influence of pH on the CQDs-MIPs fluorescence signal, phosphate solutions were used in a range between 2 and 10. Then, solutions containing 2.0mg CQDs-MIPs with different pH in the presence 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy were prepared and after 60s the response were measured at room temperature (Fig. 6 A). The maximum quenching of the fluorescence signal was observed at pH 7.0. So, pH 7.0 was chosen as an optimal pH solution. At pH >7.0, the silica shell can be ionized, and surface defects was occurred, therefore the fluorescence signal was reduced [27]. Due to the protonation of CQDs-MIPs imprinted cavities at lower pH (<2), the hydrogen bond strength between the functional monomers (APTES) and the template (PrHy) decreases. For this reason, lower pH is not favorable for the sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of CQDs-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the amount of CQDs-MIPs, solutions containing CQDs-MIPs in a range of 200–600mgL−1 in the presence 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy at pH 7.0 were prepared and the responses were recorded after 60s and at room temperature. As can be seen in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-MIPs >600mgL−1 is over than the detection ability of the spectrophotometer. Therefore, 600mgL−1 of CQDs-MIPs was selected as an optimum amount of the CQDs-MIPs.

To investigate the temperature effect (25–55°C) on the CQDs-MIPs fluorescence signal, solutions containing 600mgL−1 CQDs-MIPs at pH 7.0 in the presence 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy were prepared and after 60s the fluorescence signals were recorded. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 7 A. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-MIPs decreased with increasing the solution temperature. Therefore, room temperature was selected as an optimum temperature.

Adsorption of PrHy on the surface of CQDs-MIPs is time dependent. So, the response time of the CQDs-MIPs to PrHy was optimized. For this purpose, solutions containing 600mgL−1 CQDs-MIPs in the presence 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy at pH 7.0 were prepared. Then, the fluorescence signals, in the range of 30–180s, were recorded at room temperature. The results (Fig. 7B) showed that the response time increased with time up to 120s and then almost remained unchanged. Therefore, 120s was selected as an optimum time.

In order to investigate the sensor recovery efficiency for repeated testing, a solution containing 600mgL−1 CQDs-MIPs at pH 7.0 in the presence of 60.0μmolL−1 PrHy was prepared, and after 60s the fluorescence signal was recorded. Then, the mixture was washed with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2v/v), five-times each time with 2mL, and centrifuged to remove the template molecule to recover the CQDs-MIPs. The CQDs-MIPs was tested again with the PrHy under the same conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 8 , the recovery efficiency of the sensor has remained almost unchanged for 5 times, whereas it is noticeably reduced for more test. Therefore, this sensor can be used up to 5 times to repeat a test.

The effect of the different concentrations of PrHy on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs, CQDs-NIPs, and CQDs-MIPs are shown in Fig. 9 A–C, respectively. The CQDs have low selectivity in this determination. For example, several applications of CQDs with quantitative analysis purposes have been reported. Yue et al. have reported a method for the determination of Hg2+ using CQDs without any labeling [28]. Also, Zhang et al. have used CQDs as probes for the detection of Fe3+ ions [29]. Therefore, using of CQDs without any modification suffers from low selectivity and responds to several substances. In our work, the CQDs have been modified by MIPs and have been used for the selective determination of PrHy
As can be seen, PrHy has a better effect on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs-MIPs than CQDs-NIPs. This is due to the imprinted of PrHy on the surface of the CQDs-MIPs.

Fig. 10 A shows the Stern–Volmer (Eq. (1)) analysis, where KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and CPrHy is the concentration of PrHy:
(1) (F0-F)/F=KsvCPrHy

Also, the relationship between F0-F and PrHy concentration was plotted and results are shown in Fig. 10B. As can be seen, F0–F have a better linear relationship than (F0-F)/F with PrHy concentration. So, F0–F vs. PrHy concentration was considered as a calibration curve. The calibration equation obtained from this curve was F0–F=2.5088CPrHy +19.884 (R2 =0.9959), in the range from 2.0–250μmolL−1. The detection limit was 0.5μmolL−1 according to the definition 3Sb/m (Sb is the standard deviation of the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration equation). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for three replicate detections of 20μmolL−1 was 5.1%.

One of the most important analytical parameters is selectivity in a measurement method. Selectivity of a method refers to that it can determine a particular analyte in a complex mixture without interference from other components in the mixture. To evaluate the selectivity of method, under optimum conditions, the fluorescence signal of CQDs-MIPs to different species such as K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His), l-cysteine (Cys) and l-lysine (Lys) (all at 0.6mmolL−1) in the presence of PrHy at 60.0μmolL−1 were recorded. As shown in Fig. 11 , the selectivity of the CQDs-MIPs in the response of PrHy is much higher than CQDs-NIPs. The reason for this observation is that PrHy interacts with recognition cavities in the CQDs-MIPs while there were no imprinted cavities in the CQDs-NIPs.

To evaluate the performance of the CQDs-MIPs in PrHy detection in biological fluids such as human blood plasma, recovery method was used. Different values of PrHy were added to two samples of human blood plasma. Using standard addition method, PrHy contents were measured and the recoveries percent were calculated. The results of the determination are given in Table 1 . The recoveries of PrHy were between 96.4 and 102.3%, which show that the proposed method is suitable for PrHy detection in real samples.

In this work, an optical sensor based on CQDs coated with MIPs was used for determination of PrHy. In fact, this optical sensor detected PrHy based on the “key and lock” model. Briefly, water-soluble and fluorescent CQDs were prepared using hydrothermal carbonization of orange juice without using any chemicals. Then, the surface of the CQDs was covered with MIPs matrix via sol-gel polymerization. When the unique properties of CQDs and molecular imprinting technology combined together, the selectivity of CQDs were improved. This method has been successfully used for recovery test in human blood plasma. As shown in Table 2 , the figures of merit of this sensor with other reported methods for the determination of PrHy are compared.

RSC Adv.

Development of a simple, rapid and validated square wave voltametric method for determination of promethazine in raw material and pharmaceutical formulation using DNA modified multiwall carbon nanotube paste electrode
Sens. Actuators B

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ali A. Ensafi was graduated in Chemistry (MSc) from Shiraz University (Iran) in 1988, and he received his Ph.D. in 1991 in Analytical Chemistry at the same University. Then, he joined the Department of Chemistry at the Isfahan University of Technology (Iran). He became full professor in 2001. His research program is the development of sensors and biosensors (based electrochemical and spectroscopic methods) for diagnosis, important biological and pharmaceutical compounds, surface active agents, new energy source materials for fuel cell, supercapacitors and storage energy, using new nanocompounds.

Parisa Nasr-Esfahani received her BSc degree in Chemistry in 2011 from Isfahan University of Technology (Iran) and MSc degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2013 the same university (Iran). She is presently Ph.D. student in Analytical Chemistry at the Isfahan University of Technology (Iran). Her research interest is optical and electrochemical biosensors.

Behzad Rezaei is professor of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology. He received his BSc in chemistry from Isfahan University in 1988, his MSc in Analytical Chemistry from Isfahan University in 1991, and his Ph.D. from the Isfahan University of Technology in Analytical Chemistry in 1999. His research interests cover electrochemical, flow injection method of analysis and electrochemical sensors.
